item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do agree with Dester that SYCAMORE was acting suspiciously unprofessional, the last interview log was so wholesome it saved it for me. Upvote.

p.s. the"static on the plastic slide" bit really sent me back. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My one critique of this article is how informal SYCAMORE and Command are, and the lack of reprimand afterward. I realize MTF personnel don't have to be soulless killing machines, but the lack of professionalism in SYCAMOREs response to danger, while not unbelievable for a normal person, seems pretty out of line for Military personnel trained to handle anomalous terrain. The same goes for Command, their designation seems pretty ironic seeing the lack of command in their communication. Overall, this is the only black mark on the article really, but it is a big one. Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who can relate heavily to Poplar, I feel the emotion in this one hits well and was believably written. I also like that Sycamore was concerned for Poplar and that was also reflected in Poplar talking positively about Sycamore in his interview. The characters and emotions of the exploration logs and interview are very well done.

On a more nuts and bolts how-does-this-thing-work level I have a few unanswered questions. The first question is what happens to people who don't fit the criteria of the anomalous effects. Specifically "anomalous effects manifest when any individual who has graduated public elementary school travels into SCP-4529." This makes me think of four possible tests: 1) send a child into the forest (maybe this can be explained in the discovery log that the children from the school thought the teacher who went to their school was lying), 2) send an adult who never finished elementary school (they're rare but would probably exist in the D-Class population), 3) send someone who went to a private elementary school since the effect is said to be for those that wen to public school, and 4) send someone who was home-schooled for the elementary years. The other question is about the forest itself. How big is it and does it have entry points other than the school? Again, not every mystery needs to be answered but I think some of these are things the Foundation would try to test. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man. As someone who was bullied in elementary school, this one really strikes a chord with me. The way Poplar is written through all this is fantastic.

+1. I'd be his friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dont really know how I feel about this one, to be honest. It's a good skip, and I definitely liked that the locations and dates were actually shown unlike many others, but the exploration logs didn't do anything for me personally. I can see why some people would like it, but personally I thought they just restated the description longer, and just got in the way with a mini tale about characters I didnt have a reason to care about.

I'm gonna leave it as a no vote, because the main body is definitely good, I just personally dont like the bits stapled to the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute! I remember this! I saw this on Ideas Critique and Drafts Critique!

Anyways, I love this skip. Great job! This is your first SCP, so congratulations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dig this, congrats on your first SCP!

+ Small mistake I noticed
- Small mistake I noticed

Tau/POPLAR grunts in pain and recoils, lurching forward and .

You have an incomplete sentence in the second log :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, in terms of narrative the discovery acts as a good buffer. I'm just saying that buffer could serve more of a purpose, and provide more information that really impacts the plot. I'm getting your POI idea - someone they'd probably encounter in the exploration, right? It'd make a great set-up, but now that it's gone, it needs a change. Anyway, again, good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. It reminded me a little bit of that episode from the Twilight Zone, "Walking Distance." This site still has an outsider view of being strictly horror, and I think in some cases, newer readers and authors may still subscribe to that opinion. (Not saying it's wrong or anything!)

I'm rambling here, but the point is, I really liked this piece. Also, bonus points for incorporating my hometown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the discovery addendum is kind of a leftover from an earlier draft where I included this whole storyline about a Person of Interest, with the addendum setting that storyline up for later. I do think that removing the discovery addendum altogether wouldn't work for me, however. I do feel that including the addendum acts as a little bit of a breather for what happens next (i.e. the exploration log), and that excluding it altogether would be a little bit… abrupt. However, that's just my pretentious side speaking there.

As for the final interview stuff? I'm going to make it a little more emotionally impactful at some point, although I'm probably going to leave it be for the time being.

Either way, thank you very much for your insight, T Rutherford!

If you couldn't tell, I have a crippling addiction to the [[*user]] function. Please send help

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey wait a minute, this isn't one of my articles!

No, but honestly, fantastic work with this. You use the conprocs to effectively hook the reader, the description elaborates on a lot without beating around the bush, and the exploration / interview logs tell an engaging story.

My crit would be that the "discovery" addendum doesn't really do anything for the narrative. It just states facts we don't necessarily need. Also, I could have used a bit more prose and emotional resonance in the final interview. This person has had an intensely therapeutic experience, letting go of pain from the past and moving forward towards healthier relationships. I just don't feel that change in their tone and body language right now. It could be easily remedied with some quick changes to the dialogue, and a few additional notes on their movement / speech patterns. Illustrate for us!

Either way, +1. I'm very impressed, and hope to see more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was quite a ride. It felt pretty heartwarming for Poplar to be appreciative of Sycamore and Mahogany after he experienced a traumatizing memory at the playground at the end. I like the dialogue as well; it has really improved through time, and I think it paid off. Good work.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Welp, it's my first SCP. Here goes nothing.

A huge thanks to everyone who reviewed this SCP, including cybersqyd, Dr Whitney, and F15H35 for idea critique, and DrUnoriginal, Pedantique, and rioludoodle for draft critique. Without their work, this SCP wouldn't have been half of what it is now!
